coredata.dc:title: The role of CQDs in lithium ion sieve to recovery Li + from neutral salt lake brine
coredata.prism:publicationName: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects

Cheng, Zihong
Wang, Baodong
coredata.dc:description: The demand for lithium and its compounds is increasing by years, but separating lithium ions from brines are challenging. A novel material of Peach gum Carbon Quantum dots (PG-CQDs) lithium ion sieve was prepared by using a simple precipitation method and heat treatment. The molecular formula of the adsorbent compound was H2.05Mn1.86C0.43O4 and the maximum adsorption capacity reached 34.6 mg/g, approximately twice that of the unmodified adsorbent, with a separation factor of 293.2–11434 in brine with a high Mg/Li ratio. Experimental and theoretical analyses indicated that he presence of Mn-O-C bonds in the adsorbent and that CQDs increase the pore structure of the lithium ion sieve and that the crystallisation of the original material changes, leading to an increase in BET surface area, proton number and adsorption capacity. The outstanding material can be massively used in lithium ions extraction in complex water system and carbon quantum dots doping Li+ sieve have potential application value for extracting lithium ions.
coredata.dcterms:subject: CQDs
Salt lake brine
Li+ adsorption
Interfacial property

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: SEM micrographs (a) and EDX (b) for PG-CQDs@HMS, TEM for HMS (c) and PG-CQDs@HMS (d).

TGA-DTG graphs of HMS (a) and PG-CQDs@HMS (b), XPS spectrum of PG-CQDs@LMS Mn 2p(c), C1s (d), O1s (e) and PG-CQDs@HMS Mn 2p(f), C1s (g), O1s (h)XRD pattern of PG-CQDs@HMS and HMS (I), BET of PG-CQDs@HMS and HMS (J), FTIR parameter of CQDs, HMS, PG-CQDs@HMS (K).
Effect of PH on the adsorption capacity of adsorbents PG-CQDs@HMS and HMS (a)， effect of temperature (b) and the concentration of Li+ (c) on the adsorption capacity of adsorbents PG-CQDs@HMS.
PG-CQDs@HMS and HMS adsorption data and adsorption kinetics of PG-CQDs@HMS (initial pH at neutral in salt lake brine).
Isotherm curves of PG-CQDs@HMS at 318 K.
The possible adsorption mechanism of the PG-CQDs@HMS.
Zeta potential (a) and specific capacity (b) of PG-CQDs@HMS versus HMS after lithium adsorption.
Cyclic adsorption/desorption performance of the PG-CQDs@HMS (a), Mn dissolution rate for five cycles with 1 M HCl at a reaction time of 3 h (b).
Li+ separation performance of HMS@PG from other cations in brine.
Parameters and correlation coefficients of two kinetic models.
Li+ adsorption performance of different adsorbents.
The result of extracting Li+ from PG-CQDs@HMS with HCl.

head.ce:title: The role of CQDs in lithium ion sieve to recovery Li + from neutral salt lake brine

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry, Dalian University of Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The demand for lithium and its compounds is increasing by years, but separating lithium ions from brines are challenging. A novel material of Peach gum Carbon Quantum dots (PG-CQDs) lithium ion sieve was prepared by using a simple precipitation method and heat treatment. The molecular formula of the adsorbent compound was H2.05Mn1.86C0.43O4 and the maximum adsorption capacity reached 34.6 mg/g, approximately twice that of the unmodified adsorbent, with a separation factor of 293.2–11434 in brine with a high Mg/Li ratio. Experimental and theoretical analyses indicated that he presence of Mn-O-C bonds in the adsorbent and that CQDs increase the pore structure of the lithium ion sieve and that the crystallisation of the original material changes, leading to an increase in BET surface area, proton number and adsorption capacity. The outstanding material can be massively used in lithium ions extraction in complex water system and carbon quantum dots doping Li+ sieve have potential application value for extracting lithium ions.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The authors do not have permission to share data.

ce:section.ce:para: Lithium and its compounds have been widely applied in various industries, such as Li-ion batteries, ceramics production, aviation, aerospace industry, and the demand for lithium increasing by years[1]. The demand for lithium in these industries has increased in recent years and it is essential to find new ways to extract viable lithium resources [2]. At present, lithium and its compounds are mainly found in ores, sea water and salt lake brine[3], and brines are abundant in lithium, accounting for 70–80 % of lithium reserves in the word[4], and the extraction of lithium from brine has numerous advantages, the process is simple, inexpensive and efficient. Scholars have studied many methods to extract lithium ions from brines, such as precipitation, extraction, ion-exchange and adsorption[5,6].

In recent years, brine resources have become the preferred target for researchers to extract lithium due to the depletion of elevated-grade mineral resources[7]. The preparation of highly selective, environmentally friendly adsorbents is considered one of the most promising methods for recovering lithium from low-grade brines. In terms of the extraction of lithium from brine, there have been many reports about the extraction performance of Li+ sieve in brine (H4Mn5O12, H1.6Mn1.6O4, Magnesium-doped manganese dioxide, Fe3O4-SiO2 or H2TiO 3 and so on)[8–11]. In all of technologies for extracting lithium ions from brines, the ion-change method is an ideal approach for extracting lithium from solution with its simple craft and low price[12]. Lithium ion sieve has been recognized as the most promising lithium extraction material with memory effect[13]. Lithium ion sieve is especially suitable for extracting lithium from low concentration lithium solution[14]. However, desorption will cause manganese dissolved, thus destroying the structure of the ion screen and weakening its adsorption performance[15]. To improve the performance of extraction lithium and enhance the stability of lithium ion sieve structure, there have been reported various methods of modified ion sieve by doping element, for example, doping heavy metal elements such as Ni, Cr, Co et al. can improve the stability and performance of extraction lithium for lithium ions sieve[16]. In addition, forming a coating layer is also an important method to protect the structure. Coating metal oxide on the surface of the lithium ion sieve can inhibit the side reaction between the solution and the adsorbent[17]. The dissolution rate of manganese can be reduced by zirconium-coated lithium ion sieve [18]

CQDs are widely used in physical, biological and chemical applications due to their simplicity of preparation, optical absorption and non-toxic, environmentally friendly properties[19]. CQDs are one of the preferred targets for material modifiers due to their large specific surface area, low environmental hazard, low cost and excellent stability[20]. CQDs essentially have various tiny oxygen-containing groups (CO and C-OH) on the surface, which provide not only an electrical charge but also good active sites[21]. Biomass is widely used as a raw material for current environmentally friendly, low-cost organic materials due to its richness in groups, solubility and biocompatibility[22]. Combining CQDs with Li+ sieves can be used for the adsorption of lithium ions, an aspect that has not been studied in detail.

In our previous work, we have successfully completed the research work about the great lithium ion sieve on extracting lithium ions[23], however, there is general issue that the adsorption capacity of lithium ion sieve in neutral aqueous solution lower than alkaline environment. Therefore, there is a great need to find an original method for synthesizing a different material to adsorb Li from the neutral salt lake. Herein, the aim of this work is to enhance the Li+ adsorption capacity of Li+ sieve in the neutral salt lake brine with high ratio of magnesium to lithium, doping carbon quantum dots in lithium ions sieve via coprecipitation method. Advance instruments have been used to research the adsorption and interfacial properties for Li+ sieve, than put forward a mechanism model for doping CQDs in Li+ sieve.

Peach gum (99.0 %) was supplied by Shandong Usolf chemical technology co., ltd., China. MnCl2·4 H2O, LiOH, H2O2 (30 %), Benzene, Acetylene black, PVDF, N-methyl-2-pyrrolidone was purchased from MACKLIN (Shanghai, China), The DI water is manufactured in the laboratory. All of the agents in this study were carried out in analytical purity.
Peach gum dissolved in DI water and then reacted at 200℃ for 4 h. The reacted solution is filtered and concentrated to get modified peach gum (51.2 % virtual solid content) abbreviated as PG-CQDs.

Accurately Weighed 11.88 g MnCl2·4 H2O and 0.5 g PG-CQDs in 200 mL deionized water with 200 rpm magnetic stirring, added some LiOH and H2O2 into deionized water, then slowly dropped into the above mixed solution. A black gel was formed after continuously stirring. The obtained black gel was dried in a breaker at 60℃ and ground into a fine powder, then calcinated in air. The obtained sample stirred in 0.5 M hydrochloric acid. The acid-treatment material was filtered, washed with deionized water and dried at 60℃ in air. Finally, the material has been synthesized named PG-CQDs@HMS. In the same synthesis condition, the pure material without PG-CQDs denoted as HMS. The preparation process is shown in Fig. 1.

Li+ adsorption kinetics and adsorption equilibrium experiments were researched by stirring (120 r/min) PG-CQDs@HMS or HMS (10 mg) in 100 mL salt lake brine solutions (initial Li+ concentrations is 221.43 mg/L and pH at neutral) for 24 h at room temperatures; Equilibrium adsorption capacity (Qe) of PG-CQDs@HMS was calculated by Eq.(1) [24].
(1)
Q
e
=
C
0
−
C
f
∙ V
m
Where Q e is the mass of metal ions adsorbed per gram of PG-CQDs@HMS at equilibrium (mg/g), cf is the concentration of metal ions at equilibrium (mg/L), C0 is the initial concentration of lithium ions (mg/L), V is the solution volume (mL), and m is the mass of PG-CQDs@HMS adsorbent (g).

The balance between adsorbing time and lithium ions adsorption capacity reflects the saturated adsorption capacity of PG-CQDs@HMS under the operating conditions. Data analysis is required to fit different isothermal adsorption models to determine the model that best fits the adsorption process. The adsorption equilibrium equations are shown in Eq. (2) and Eq. (3). [25]
(2)
q
t
=
q
m
∙ ( 1 − exp ( −
k
1
∙ t ) )
(3)
q
t
=
k
2
∙
q
m
2
∙ t
1 +
k
2
∙
q
m
∙ t
Where k1 (h−1), k2 (g·mg−1·h−1), q m (mg·g−1) and q t (mg·g−1) is the rate constant of first-order adsorption, the rate constant of second-order adsorption, the adsorption capacity in theory and the actual adsorption capacity, respectively.

Li+ competitive adsorption was conducted by stirring (120 r/min) PG-CQDs@HMS samples (0.1 g) in 50 mL of the same solution at the same temperatures. The separation efficiency of PG-CQDs@HMS was evaluated in terms of distribution coefficient (Kd) and the separation factor (α) according to Eqs. (4)-(5) [26].
(4)
K
d
=
(
C
0
−
C
e
) ∙ V
C
Me
∙ m
(5)
α =
K
d
Li
K
d
Me

Which Me is Li+, Na+, K+, Mg2+; C0 is the initial concentration (mg·L−1) of the metal ions; CMe is the final concentrations (mg·L−1) of Li+, Na+, K+, Mg2+; m is the mass of PG-CQDs@HMS (g); V is salt lake brine volume (mL).

Accurately weighing 70 mg PG-CQDs@HMS, 20 mg acetylene black, 10 mg PVDF, which were added in 200 μL N-methyl-2-pyrrolidone (NMP) and adequately mixing. The mixture was coated onto a nickel foam in 1 cm2, and dried at 60℃ overnight, the mixture loading was 14.3 mg. Galvanostatic charge-discharge (GCD) measurements were performed on an electrochemical workstation (Shanghai Chenhua, CHI 660D). The specific capacity was calculated from chronopotentiometric curves according to Eq. (6) [27].
(6)
C
m
=
2 ∙
i
m
∙
∫
Vdt
V
2
I
V
0
V
t
Where V is the initial (V0) and final (Vt) potential value, m is the mass (g) of the active and im = I/m (A·g−1) is the constant discharge current.

X-ray diffraction (XRD) is determined the composition and structure with a Cu Kα (λ = 1.54 Å) source at 20 kV and 200 mA in a D/Max2400 (Rigaku Co., Japan). The transmission electron microscopic (TEM) research was carried out on a JEOL 2200FS at 200 kV. N 2 adsorption experiments were conducted using an 3 H-2000PS1 Surface Area Analyzer (Beishide Instrument Technology (Beijing) Co., Ltd, China). Zeta potential was conducted by ZetaPALS/90plus (America). Surface energy heterogeneity of powders was measured using a Surface Energy Analyser (SEA, Surface Measurement Systems Ltd., London, UK). Atomic absorption spectrophotometer (Solan 969，USA) was used for determination of metal concentrations. Hydrogen content was measured by Elemental Analyzer (Vario EL, Germany). Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) was carried on the samples using a TG 209 F1 Libra system (Netzsch，Germany). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was conducted with two separate systems equipped with monochromatic Al K sources (ESCALab250Xi, USA) to analyze the chemical composition of the samples. FTIR was performed on an Alpha Infrared Spectrometer (Bruker, Germany).

As shown in the SEM image in Fig. 2 a，the prepared PG-CQDs@HMS has uniformity of size and shape. According to the EDX analysis, no additional element was detected except C, O and Mn for the PG-CQDs@HMS and the weight of C, O, Mn are 2.97 %, 36.89 % and 58.96 %, respectively shown in Fig. 2 b. The results indicate that carbons were successfully doped into lithium ion sieve[28] (the spectrogram occurs silicon peak due to silicon chip as the sample carrier). In addition, the results of TEM for PG-CQDs@HMS and HMS are shown in Fig. 2 c-d. HMS has a uniform size about 15 nm shown in Fig. 2 c. Fig. 2 d illustrates PG-CQDs has been synthesized without change in shape, but the size of PG-CQDs at 18–30 nm.

To further determine the presence of PG-CQDs for PG-CQDs@HMS, TGA analysis is shown in Fig. 3 a-b. The weight loss before 200℃ for HMS and PG-CQDs@HMS are 3.5 % and 4 % due to absorbed water. The weight loss was evident from 200 to 450℃, due to crystal water. The low residual weight for PG-CQDs@HMS is a greater proportion of carbon due to the presence of PG-CQDs. The results are consistent with SEM and TEM.

For further study, the chemical bonding states for PG-CQDs@LMS and PG-CQDs@HMS were analyzed by XPS measurements, and the results of Mn (2p), O (1 s), C (1 s) are shown in Fig. 3 c-h. The Mn2p XPS spectrum of PG-CQDs@LMS (Fig. 3 c) exhibits two peaks at 653.95 eV (Mn 2p1/2) and 642.2 eV (Mn 2p3/2). The energy separation of 11.75 eV illustrating +3 Mn and +4 Mn exists in PG-CQDs@LMS, The spin energy separation of Mn 2p3/2 and Mn 2p1/2 in PG-CQDs@HMS (Fig. 3 f), located at 642.2 and 654.0 eV, respectively, is 11.8 eV, indicating the +4 oxidation state for Mn in PG-CQDs@HMS[29], therefore, the structure of PG-CQDs@HMS keep stable in the process of intercalation and de-intercalation for lithium ions[30]. The C1 s deconvolution spectra of PG-CQDs@LMS, PG-CQDs@HMS was analyzed by curve fitting. The C1 s peaks of PG-CQDs@LMS (Fig. 3 d) in spectrum show the components of the carbon bond, 284.8 eV (Graphitic carbon), 286.26 eV (C-O), 288.80 eV (O-CO), 292.18 eV (π-π transition)[31]. Fig. 3 g shows the peak of 292.18 eV (π-π transition) disappeared in PG-CQDs@HMS, where the intensity of C1s at 288.56 eV (O-CO) decreased. Fig. 3 e shows the peak of O1s located at 529.79 eV (Mn-O), 531.93 eV (O-H and C-O), 532.75 eV (CO) in the PG-CQDs@LMS. The intensity of O1s spectrum peaks on PG-CQDs@HMS (Fig. 3 h) located at 529.99 eV (Mn-O) increased, the intensity of O1s at 531.36 eV (C-O) and 533.52 eV (CO) have decreased. The information of C1s and O1s illustrates that the C-O-Mn bonds exist in PG-CQDs@HMS[32]. The powder X-ray diffraction patterns of the samples were shown in Fig. 3 I. The PG-CQDs@HMS different from HMS, its diffraction peak stronger than HMS, and the peaks of (311) and (400) have slightly moved to low diffraction angle and the crystal lattice distortion rate increased a little[33]. The porosity of PG-CQDs@HMS and HMS is researched by nitrogen adsorption and the adsorption isotherms are shown in Fig. 3 g. The isotherm of the HMS (red circle curve) and PG-CQDs@HMS exhibit combined characteristics of type II and IV that belong to micro-mesoporous structure[34]. The PG-CQDs@HMS has greater surface area (84 m2/g) and total pore volume (37.6 mm 3/g) than HMS (67 m2/g, 28.9 mm3/g) with BJH and DFT models.

The FTIR spectrum of PG-CQDs, PG-CQDs@HMS, HMS is shown in Fig. 3 k. The peak at 3421 cm −1, 2928 cm −1, 1721 cm −1, 1616 cm −1, 1425 cm −1 and 1070 cm −1, belong to -OH, C-H, CO, CC stretching, -OH distorting and C-O stretching, respectively[35]. The results suggest that the PG-CQDs contain numerous carboxyl and hydroxyl groups[36] and PG-CQDs@HMS and HMS have same functional groups, but the peak of intensity on PG-CQDs@HMS located at 904 cm −1 (the intercrystalline proton vibration peak) is obviously stronger than HMS due to additional active sites for ions exchange (Li+ to H+) for PG-CQDs@HMS. The spectrum of the HMS shows two peaks at 516 cm−1 and 604 cm−1, suggesting the asymmetric stretching modes of MnO6 groups[37]. Nevertheless, the shift in the spectrum peaks of at 476 cm−1 and 584 cm−1 and slight peak at 1050 cm−1 implies that Mn-O-C is present in the PG-CQDs@HMS.

As shown in Fig. 4, adsorption capacity tests of PG-CQDs@HMS and HMS at different pH, temperature and the concentration of Li+ were investigated to understand the adsorption performance of for Li+. It can be recognized that the adsorption capacity becomes significant with increasing pH for the unmodified HMS due to protonation of the active site of the adsorbent at lower pH and a reduction in the number of surface binding sites[38]. Whereas, the maximum adsorption capacity (34.6 mg/g) is exhibited under neutral conditions (pH=7), which is better than the acidic and basic conditions for PG-CQDs@HMS in Fig. 4 a. The result can be attributed to the presence of a substantial amount of OH and COOH in the surface of PG-CQDs@HMS，which increases the exchange sites for Li+. Under neutral conditions, the modified adsorbent PG-CQDs@HMS had a larger adsorption capacity compared to the unmodified adsorbent HMS. However, it is the reason why the adsorption capacity of PG-CQDs@HMS is lower than HMS that the surface protons of CQDs tend to interact with OH- in alkaline solution, reducing the ion exchange interaction of CQDs with Li+. PG-CQDs@HMS adsorbent is more favorable for use under neutral conditions.

In view of the complexity of the brine composition in the salt lake, the sorption selectivity of PG-CQDs@HMS between Li+ and competing cations including Na+, K+, Ca2+ and Mg2+ needed to be investigated. The effects of studying the adsorption capacities of competing cations at 278 K, 288 K, 298 K, 308 K and 318 K, respectively, are shown in Fig. 4 b. For all cations, the adsorption capacity increases with increasing temperature because the ion exchange reaction during the adsorption process leads to changes in the structure of the adsorbent at the interface resulting in an entropy increase. And according to previous literature studies, the ion adsorption capacity increases with increasing temperature[39]. Nevertheless, it is considerably greater for the adsorption capacity Li+ than for the remaining ions.

Different dilutions of binary solutions including Li+ and Me (Me = Na+, K+, Ca2+ and Mg2+) mixed at 298 K. The experiments were carried out and the adsorption capacity of Li+ and the separation factor
α
Me
Li
were determined and the results are shown in Fig. 4 c and Table 1. The results of distribution coefficient (K d), separation factor (
α
Me
Li
) of Li+ in salt lake brine are shown in Table 4. The equilibrium distribution coefficients (K d) of the coexisting metal ions were considerably inferior than Li+, and the separation factor (
α
Me
Li
) values (293.2–11434) of Li+ to other coexisting ions much greater than 1, indicating PG-CQDs@HMS maintain strong selectivity for lithium ions in salt lake brine. The relatively superior selectivity could well be interpreted by the ion sieve effect, the material has a three dimensional (1×3) tunnel consisting of hollow 8a tetrahedron and 16c octahedron, and the tunnel size suited Li+ and H+ exchange, but coexisting ions (K+, Na+) have higher ionic radius, and the hydration energy of Mg 2+ higher than Li+.[40] The results demonstrate elevated concentrate coexist ions (K+, Na+, Mg2+) do not disturb the extraction of Li+ during ion-exchange process. The result can be attributed to the low concentration of lithium ions in the brine and its weak mass transfer kinetics, the mechanism of Li+ in the adsorbent is ion exchange rather than simple adsorption for additional impurity ions (Ca2+, Mg2+, Na+, K+). Therefore, the lithium ion sieve adsorbent has a high selectivity for Li+.

The Li+ adsorption kinetics of PG-CQDs@HMS in the presence of different competing ions was investigated to understand the Li+ adsorption performance in brine as shown in Fig. 5 and
Table 2. It can be confirmed from Fig. 5 a that HMS and PG-CQDs@HMS have different sorption capacities for different ions, with PG-CQDs@HMS exhibiting a significantly higher Li sorption capacity (maximum sorption capacity of 34.6 mg/L) and a slightly lower sorption capacity for competing ions shown in Fig. 5 b. Equilibrium was reached within 10 h using PG-CQDs@HMS, with a smaller adsorption capacity for other ions. Based on the fitting results in Table 2, pseudo-second-order (R2 = 0.991) has an advantage over pseudo-first-order (R2 = 0.967) for the description of the Li+ adsorption process, reflecting that chemisorption would be the controlling step and the adsorption rate is mainly controlled by the stoichiometric exchange of H+ and Li+ [41].

The adsorption isotherms at a temperature of 318 K are shown in Fig. 6. It can be seen that the adsorption enhances with increasing temperature, indicating that the Li+ adsorption process is a heat absorption reaction shown in Fig. 4 b. Furthermore, according to the correlation coefficients fitted in Fig. 6, the adsorption Langmiur isotherm model (R2 = 0.99) was better than the Freundlich isotherm model (R2 = 0.97) for the adsorption of Li+ in PG-CQDs@HMS. the Qmax values calculated by the Langmiur model were in general agreement with the trend of the Qe values in the experimental data.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The study of Li+ adsorption mechanism
ce:section.ce:para:
Synthesize procedure and adsorption mechanism models of PG-CQDs@HMS displayed in Fig. 7, according to Elemental analyzer, EDX, Atomic Absorption spectrophotometer, and XPS etc illustrate CQDs have been successfully doped into lithium ion sieve, and CQDs linked with lithium ion sieve though Mn-O-C bonds, the molecular formula of PG-CQDs@HMS can be seen as H2.05Mn1.86C0.43O4 (average valence state of carbon is 3.47), the existed hydroxy and carboxylic promote to capture lithium ions for PG-CQDs@HMS, relevant chemical reactions show at Eqs. (7)-(9). Besides doping CQDs have increased the pore size, BET surface area, surface free energy and electrochemical properties beneficial to adsorbing lithium ions, the special micropore structure helps PG-CQDs@HMS keep higher selectivity in adsorption process.
(7) H2.05Mn1.86C0.43O4 + xLi+→H2.05-x LixMn1.86C0.43O4 + xH +
(8) Mn-O-H + Li+→Mn-O-Li + H +
(9) Mn-O-C(O-H) 3-x + (3-x) Li+→Mn-O-C(O-Li) 3-x + (3-x) H + (x=1, 2)

It indicates the surface of PG-CQDs@HMS and HMS have negative charge in surface in Fig. 8 a. Zeta potential have decreased with the lithium ion concentration increased, Zeta potential of the material PG-CQDs@HMS is higher than HMS after Li+ exchange, demonstrate the numbers of lithium ions insert electric double layer of PG-CQDs@HMS more than the pure material, and the adsorption achieve a state of equilibrium, the number of lithium ions insert electric double layer resulting in zeta potential rise, which is similar to our previous study[42]. The main reason is that the modified material has larger surface area, which the number of lithium ions in the double layer increased. Besides, the figure also illustrates that PG-CQDs@HMS has higher surface free energy, demonstrate modified sample more tend to adsorbing lithium ions. Fig. 8 b shows the specific capacity of PG-CQDs@HMS and HMS reached at 205 F·g −1 and 148 F·g −1, relatively, demonstrates CQDs have improved lithium ion sieve capacitive property, so that increased the charge sites for adsorbing lithium ions.[43]

To validate the stability of the adsorbent, we investigated the effect of various hydrochloric acid concentrations and different washing times on the lithium extraction rate and the manganese dissociation rate using CQDs modified with Li+ sieves. Table 4 shows the values obtained for the pickling via four concentrations of hydrochloric acid and two times of agitation. It can be seen throughout the results that the lithium extraction rate and manganese dissolution rate rose with increasing hydrochloric acid concentration. Moreover, the lithium extraction mainly occurs at the beginning of 3 h when the lithium dissolution has fundamentally reached the equilibrium with pickling at 24 h. The reaction time of 24 h with 0.5 M HCl resulted in the identical lithium extraction rates and manganese dissolution rates as at the reaction time of 3 h with 1 M HCl. Consequently, cyclic test conditions were performed with 1 M HCl at a reaction time of 3 h.

To verify the recyclability of the adsorbent, a cycling adsorption/desorption test was carried out by using PG-CQDs@HMS and the desorption process was regenerated by immersing the adsorbent in 1 M hydrochloric acid for 3 h, repeated three times. During the five experiments of cycling, the maximum adsorption capacity decreased from 34.8 mg/g to 30.2 mg/g and after five adsorptions, the adsorption capacity was still 86.8 % of the original maximum adsorption capacity shown in Fig. 9 a. The results show that the adsorbent PG-CQDs@HMS has excellent practical value. In addition，PG-CQDs@HMS was also evaluated by the dissolution loss of Mn. As shown in Fig. 9 b, Mn loss was below 0.88 % from the first to the fifth cycle. The result indicates that synthesized PG-CQDs@HMS possesses excellent recycling performance for Li+ recovery.

In summary, a modified Li+ adsorbent PG-CQDs@HMS was obtained by co-precipitation of CQDs embedded in a Li+ sieve by using peach gum as a raw material in this study. The molecular formula of the adsorbent compound was H2.05Mn1.86C0.43O4 and the maximum adsorption capacity reached 34.6 mg/g, approximately twice that of the unmodified adsorbent, with a separation factor of 293.2–11434 in brine with a high Mg/Li ratio (pH neutral). The adsorption kinetics showed that the adsorption process of PG-CQDs@HMS was pseudo-second-order and mainly controlled by chemisorption. Results from BET, X-ray diffraction (XRD), zeta potential, specific capacity and surface free energy indicate the presence of Mn-O-C bonds in the adsorbent and that CQDs increase the pore structure of the lithium ion sieve and that the crystallisation of the original material changes, leading to an increase in BET surface area, proton number and adsorption capacity. This work has potential applications for the adsorption of lithium ions from salt lakes.

Zhe Zhang: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing – original draft. Yuzhen Pan: Investigation. Lei Wu: Resources, Supervision. Jiali Wang: Resources. Yawen Sun: Resources. Linsen Wei: Resources. Zhen Chen: Resources. Wei Ma: Funding acquisition, Writing – review & editing. Baodong Wang: Project administration. Zihong Cheng: Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge funding support from the National Major Projects (DUT17ZD225), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21677027) and Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (DUT21ZD202).
